alternatives_considered: Keep the rule as-is alongside the lifecycle field — redundant, the schema already enforces it
Remove lifecycle field and keep the rule — loses the benefit of automated staleness detection
content.context: The old rule required agents to mentally classify memories as 'state' (ages fast) or 'learning' (ages slowly). With the KB schema, every doc has a lifecycle field that encodes this classification. Scripts (check-stale.sh) can enforce staleness thresholds automatically: state > 30 days = stale, learning > 90 days = stale, permanent = never stale.
content.decision: The state-vs-learning rule is replaced by the lifecycle field (permanent/learning/state) in the KB schema. The architecture enforces structurally what the rule enforced behaviorally.
impact: state-vs-learning.md rule is retired — no longer loaded at boot
check-stale.sh enforces the same principle at zero token cost
Agents must still think about lifecycle when creating docs, but the classification is structural, not a behavioral rule to remember
content.why: The old rule required agents to mentally classify memories as 'state' (ages fast) or 'learning' (ages slowly). With the KB schema, every doc has a lifecycle field that encodes this classification. Scripts (check-stale.sh) can enforce staleness thresholds automatically: state > 30 days = stale, learning > 90 days = stale, permanent = never stale.
